Lagundi.name: Lagundi
Lagundi.description: Lagundi (Vitex negundo) is one of the ten medicinal plants officially endorsed by the Philippine Department of Health (DOH) for its proven effectiveness in treating respiratory ailments. It is a shrub that grows up to 5 meters tall with distinctive five-fingered leaves and small blue to lavender flowers.
medicinal_uses: Relieves cough and asthma (DOH-approved for respiratory ailments)
Acts as expectorant for productive cough
preparation_steps: Collect fresh or dried Lagundi leaves
Wash thoroughly in clean water
Boil 2-3 tablespoons of dried leaves OR 1 cup fresh leaves in 2 cups water for 10-15 minutes
Let it cool and strain the liquid
Drink as tea, 1 cup 3 times daily after meals
For cough relief, add honey to taste
For children: Use half the adult dosage
warnings: Consult doctor if symptoms persist after 3 days
May cause allergic reactions in some people
Keep out of reach of children when not in use
Lagundi.cultural_relevance: Lagundi has been used in Philippine traditional medicine for centuries. It is one of the ten herbal medicines endorsed by the Department of Health (DOH) for its proven medicinal properties. The plant is commonly found in backyards and is often the first remedy for cough and colds in Filipino households.

Sambong.name: Sambong
Sambong.description: Sambong (Blumea balsamifera) is officially recognized by the Philippine DOH for its diuretic properties and effectiveness in treating kidney stones. It is a coarse erect annual herb that grows up to 1.5 meters tall with soft, hairy leaves that emit a camphor-like scent when crushed.
preparation_steps: Collect fresh Sambong leaves, preferably mature ones
Wash leaves thoroughly in clean water to remove dirt
Boil 1 cup of chopped fresh leaves (or 1/2 cup dried) in 3 cups water
Simmer for 15-20 minutes over low heat
Strain and let cool to room temperature
Drink 1 cup, 3 times daily after meals
For external use: Use decoction as poultice for wounds
warnings: Consult doctor before use with other medications
Not for long-term use without medical supervision
Pregnant women should consult a physician before use
Sambong.cultural_relevance: Sambong is officially recognized by the Philippine Department of Health as a medicinal plant for its diuretic properties and effectiveness in treating kidney stones. It has been used by traditional healers (albularyo) for generations and remains a popular herbal remedy in rural communities.

Bayabas.name: Bayabas
Bayabas.description: Bayabas or Guava (Psidium guajava) is one of the most important traditional medicines in the Philippines, endorsed by DOH for treating diarrhea and wounds. It is a small evergreen tree that grows up to 10 meters tall with smooth, oblong leaves and produces edible fruits.
medicinal_uses: Relieves toothache when leaves are chewed
Treats mouth ulcers and gum problems
preparation_steps: For diarrhea: Collect 10-15 fresh Bayabas leaves, wash thoroughly
Boil in 2 cups water for 10-15 minutes until water turns brown
Strain and let cool
Drink 1 cup every 4-6 hours until diarrhea stops
For wound wash: Boil a handful of leaves until water is dark brown
Cool and use to wash affected area 2-3 times daily
For mouthwash: Gargle with cooled boiled leaf water
For toothache: Chew 2-3 young leaves directly
warnings: Pregnant women should consult doctor first
Use clean leaves to avoid infection
Bayabas.cultural_relevance: Bayabas is ubiquitous in the Philippines, found in almost every backyard. It is one of the most important traditional medicines, especially for treating diarrhea in children. The leaves are readily available year-round, making it an accessible first-aid remedy in rural areas.

Ampalaya.name: Ampalaya
Ampalaya.description: Ampalaya or Bitter Melon (Momordica charantia) is widely used in the Philippines for managing diabetes. The DOH recognizes its anti-diabetic properties. It is a climbing vine that produces distinctive warty fruits that are bitter but highly medicinal.
preparation_steps: Wash and slice fresh Ampalaya fruit or collect young leaves
For tea: Boil 1 cup sliced leaves or fruit in 2 cups water for 15 minutes
Alternatively, juice fresh leaves and mix with equal amount of water
Drink 1/2 cup, twice daily before meals
For cooking: Can be cooked as vegetable dish (ginisang ampalaya with egg)
Seeds can be eaten (remove red covering) for additional benefits
Dried leaves can be powdered and stored for future use
warnings: Diabetics should monitor blood sugar closely when using
Pregnant women should avoid (may cause miscarriage)
May cause stomach upset in large amounts
Do not take with diabetes medication without doctor's advice
Bitter taste may be strong - honey can be added
Ampalaya.cultural_relevance: Ampalaya is a staple vegetable in Filipino cuisine and medicine. The Department of Health recognizes its anti-diabetic properties. Despite its bitter taste, it is valued for its numerous health benefits and is often used in the traditional dish 'ginisang ampalaya.'
